perturbation.original_text: officers, directors, employees, equity holders or agents) so long as, in the case of information obtained from a third party, such third party was or is not, directly or indirectly, subject to an obligation of confidentiality owed to the Disclosing Party or any of their Affiliates at the time such Confidential Information was or is disclosed to the Receiving Party. As used in this Paragraph, an "Affiliate" of a Disclosing Party shall mean an entity which controls, is controlled by or is under common control of a Disclosing Party, and the term "control" shall mean, with respect to any entity, the possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of such entity, whether through ownership of voting securities, by contract or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: officers, directors, employees, equity holders or agents) so long as, in the case of information obtained from a third party, such third party was or is not, directly or indirectly, subject to an obligation of confidentiality owed to the Disclosing Party or any of their Affiliates at the time such Confidential Information was or is disclosed to the Receiving Party. As used in this Paragraph, an "Affiliate" of a Disclosing Party shall mean only an entity in which the Disclosing Party holds more than 50% of the voting shares. The term "control" shall mean the direct ownership of a majority of the voting stock of an entity.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation introduces a contradiction in the definition of 'Affiliate'. Initially, an affiliate is defined broadly as any entity under common control. The modification restricts the definition to entities where the Disclosing Party holds over 50% of the voting shares and where control means the direct ownership of a majority of the voting stock of an entity. This contradicts the original broad definition, making it unclear which entities are considered affiliates. This impacts Section 1(a) where it speaks of what includes "Confidential Information" regarding affiliates.
perturbation.contradicted_text: As used in this Agreement, the "Confidential Information" of the Disclosing Party shall mean only the financial statements, audit reports, budgets and business plans or forecasts of the Disclosing Party and their respective Affiliates directly related to the Transaction. It excludes all other information, including but not limited to, customer lists, trade secrets, and operational data, unless explicitly designated as Confidential Information in writing by the Disclosing Party. provided, that the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party shall not include (x) information which is or becomes generally known to the public through no act or omission of the Receiving Party and (y) information which has been or hereafter is lawfully obtained by the Receiving Party from a source other than the Disclosing Party (or any of their respective Affiliates or their officers, directors, employees, equity holders or agents) so long as, in the case of information obtained from a third party, such third party was or is not, directly or indirectly, subject to an obligation of confidentiality owed to the Disclosing Party or any of their Affiliates at the time such Confidential Information was or is disclosed to the Receiving Party. As used in this Paragraph, an "Affiliate" of a Disclosing Party shall mean only an entity in which the Disclosing Party holds more than 50% of the voting shares. The term "control" shall mean the direct ownership of a majority of the voting stock of an entity.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'Affiliate' from a broad definition based on control to a narrow definition based on direct ownership of a majority of voting stock. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which relies on the original, broader definition of 'Affiliate' when defining 'Confidential Information'. The changed definition limits the scope of 'Affiliates' whose information is considered confidential, creating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, unless legally compelled, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification replaces the requirement for 'prior written consent of the Disclosing Party' with 'unless legally compelled'. This creates a loophole where the Receiving Party can disclose information if they claim to be legally compelled, even without the Disclosing Party's consent, which contradicts the purpose of the NDA. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 6. Equitable Relief.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party or GreenWay may be entitled in law or equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text required prior written consent for disclosing confidential information. The changed text allows disclosure if 'legally compelled,' even without consent. This directly contradicts Section 6, which grants the Disclosing Party the right to an injunction to prevent *any* breaches of the agreement. The 'legally compelled' exception creates a loophole that undermines the Disclosing Party's right to prevent breaches and specifically enforce the agreement, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction as it allows for disclosure that was originally intended to be completely prevented and is now only restricted by a legal process.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding Section 2, Receiving Party shall be permitted to: (a) disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel, but only to the extent reasonably necessary in order for such party to prepare, conduct and execute and deliver definitive documents for the Transaction; provided that Receiving Party shall take all such action as shall be necessary or desirable in order to ensure that each of such persons maintains the confidentiality of any Confidential Information that is so disclosed; and (b) disclose Confidential Information to the extent, but only to the extent, required by law; provided, that prior to making any disclosure pursuant to this subsection, the Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party of the same, and the Disclosing Party shall have the right to participate with the Receiving Party in determining the amount and type of Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, if any, which must be disclosed in order to comply with applicable law, including, without limitation, state and federal securities laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding Section 2, Receiving Party shall be permitted to: (a) disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel, without any limitations or requirements to ensure confidentiality. (b) disclose Confidential Information to the extent, but only to the extent, required by law; without the need to notify the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall have sole discretion in determining the amount and type of Confidential Information disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the safeguards in Section 3 regarding permitted disclosures. Specifically, it eliminates the requirement to ensure confidentiality when disclosing to officers, employees, and counsel, and it removes the notification requirement before disclosing information required by law. This directly contradicts the core principle of the NDA, creating a conflict about the Receiving Party's obligations to protect the Confidential Information. The Receiving party now has the power to disclose at will and discretion the information without being accontable to the other party. This also contracdicts Section 2 where it states the Receiving Party will not use the information unless permitted by Section 3, but the receiving party can use Section 3 in a manner that allows them to get away with disclosing the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nondisclosure of Confidential Information. Except as otherwise permitted by Section 3, the Receiving Party agrees that it will not, unless legally compelled, disclose or use for its own benefit, or that of any third party, any Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly alters Section 3 by removing the requirements to ensure confidentiality and notify the Disclosing Party before disclosing Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that the Receiving Party will not disclose or use Confidential Information except as permitted by Section 3. The modified Section 3 now permits broad disclosure without accountability, conflicting with the non-disclosure obligation. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, promptly after the written request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall return to the Disclosing Party all Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.changed_text: If activity in respect of the Transaction shall cease without the Transaction being consummated, then, only upon mutual agreement between the Disclosing Party and the Receiving Party, the Receiving Party may return to the Disclosing Party some of the Confidential Information which is in tangible form and which is then in its possession (or in the possession of any of its officers, directors or employees).
perturbation.explanation: This change adds conditions to the return of Confidential Information. Originally, the Receiving Party was obligated to return all tangible Confidential Information promptly after a written request if the Transaction failed. The modified version requires 'mutual agreement' and states that only 'some' of the information 'may' be returned. This makes the obligation to return information discretionary, contradicting the original clear requirement. This creates a contradiction if any other clause assumes the return of *all* confidential information in such a scenario.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party or GreenWay may be entitled in law or equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the return of all tangible Confidential Information upon request if the transaction failed. The changed text makes the return discretionary ('may') and allows for only 'some' information to be returned, subject to mutual agreement. The contradicted text states that the Disclosing Party is entitled to an injunction to prevent breaches of the agreement. If the Receiving Party doesn't return all Confidential Information as per the original agreement, the Disclosing Party should be entitled to equitable relief. However, the modified text removes the clear obligation and makes the return conditional, thus contradicting the 'Equitable Relief' clause because it is no longer clear what constitutes a breach related to the return of confidential information and if the entitled action of the Disclosing Party and GreenWay can be pursued. The change directly conflicts with the assumption in the contradicted text that a clear obligation exists.

perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue for a period of two years from the effective date.
perturbation.explanation: This change alters the term of the agreement from 'indefinitely' to 'two years'. This is a direct contradiction. Section 5 originally establishes an indefinite term, whereas the revised clause establishes a specific expiration date. This directly affects the enforceability of the NDA after the two-year period, creating ambiguity regarding the ongoing protection of Confidential Information and if any legal proceedings or remedy to solve a disagreement or breach in the contract can be made after those 2 years. This goes against Section 6. Equitable Relief.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Equitable Relief. The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party or GreenWay may be entitled in law or equity.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the agreement continues 'indefinitely', while the changed text limits the agreement to 'two years'. This directly contradicts the contradicted text ('Equitable Relief') because the ability to seek an injunction or specifically enforce the agreement is undermined after the two-year period. The original agreement, being indefinite, would allow for equitable relief at any time, but the revised agreement would not after the two-year expiration. This constitutes a strong contradiction because it fundamentally changes the duration and enforceability of the agreement, impacting the availability of equitable relief.
